title: The Iceberg Below the Waterline: Systems Thinking and the Art of Seeing Whole
summary: Every ministry leader has encountered the experience of solving a problem only to watch it reappear in a different form six months later. Or of implementing a strategy that seemed sound but produced unexpected — and damaging — consequences

blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
Every ministry leader has encountered the experience of solving a problem only to watch it reappear in a different form six months later. Or of implementing a strategy that seemed sound but produced unexpected — and damaging — consequences in other parts of the organization or community. Or of seeing a conflict escalate despite everyone's apparent good intentions. These experiences are not evidence of bad leadership; they are evidence that the leader is managing events without seeing the underlying system that produces them. This session introduces Systems Thinking — the discipline of seeing wholes, patterns, and structures rather than isolated events — as the essential complement to all the personal and organizational development work of the prior twelve sessions.

The Systems Iceberg model (adapted from Daniel Kim and Peter Senge) is the session's anchor tool. The iceberg has five levels: Events (what we observe on the surface), Patterns (when events are consistent and recurring), Structures (the habits, norms, policies, and relationships driving the patterns), Mental Models (the deeply held beliefs and assumptions that create and sustain the structures), and Intent (the foundational purpose or goal that shapes the mental models). Most leaders — and most organizations — spend their energy reacting to Events while the Iceberg's massive substructure remains invisible and unaddressed. Breakthrough requires diving below the waterline: identifying the Mental Models and Structures that produce unwanted patterns and designing interventions at those deeper levels.

The session also introduces the After-Action Review (AAR) framework — a six-step collective learning process developed in military contexts and adapted for community ministry by Senge — as a practical tool for teams to examine what happened, why it differed from what was intended, what they learned, and how to move forward with a shared vision. The Building on Past Success case study — the Jackson Youth Outreach 3-on-3 Basketball Tournament that grows from a focused, community-rooted initiative into a sprawling event that loses its missional core — provides a compelling application of both the Iceberg and the AAR: the events (declining commitments, budget overruns, community opposition) make sense only when the underlying structural drift from community development to entertainment production is made visible.
blocks.html:
Session 13

The Iceberg Below the Waterline

Systems Thinking and the Art of Seeing Whole — why every ministry problem you have solved twice was solved at the wrong level

Pastor Randall Jones stood at the front of the room holding a single sheet of paper — the financial summary for the Jackson Youth Outreach 3-on-3 Basketball Tournament. Four years ago, he had championed this project. It had started as a lean, community-rooted outreach to teenage boys who would never walk through a church door on their own. In Year One, ten young men prayed a first-time commitment prayer after a simple closing message. The room celebrated. By Year Four, 2,000 people were crowding the Saturday night concert, a local radio station was broadcasting live, and the deficit was $10,000. Commitments: ten — exactly the same as Year One, despite a crowd two hundred times larger.

Nobody intended this. Nobody woke up on any single morning and decided to transform a ministry into an entertainment production. The drift happened gradually, invisibly, driven by forces no one named and no one saw clearly enough to address. That invisibility is not a failure of intention. It is a failure of seeing — a failure to look below the waterline at the structures, mental models, and drifting intent that were silently reshaping everything above the surface. This session is about learning to see differently.

Why Leaders Keep Solving the Same Problem Twice

Every experienced ministry leader can recall a variation of this experience: a conflict resolved, only to reemerge in a different relationship six months later. A funding crisis addressed, only to return when a key donor steps back. A program redesigned, only to produce the same attendance patterns as before. Peter Senge identified this phenomenon as one of the defining characteristics of organizations that have not yet learned to think systemically — they treat events as if they were causes rather than symptoms (Senge, 1990).

Senge's foundational argument in The Fifth Discipline is that most organizational problems do not originate in the events we observe but in the deeper structures that produce them. When leaders react only to events — patching the symptom without understanding the underlying dynamic — the problem invariably resurfaces, often in a more entrenched form. He calls this event-level thinking: the reflexive tendency to see an isolated occurrence, respond to it, declare it resolved, and move on, never asking what structural force created the event in the first place (Senge, 1990).

This is not a leadership failure unique to poor leaders. It is actually the natural default for intelligent, well-intentioned people under pressure — people very much like the JYO organizing team. As Donella Meadows argued in Thinking in Systems, "The behavior of a system cannot be known just by knowing the elements of which the system is made" (Meadows, 2008, p. 12). What drives behavior is structure — the relationships among elements, the feedback loops operating between them, the policies and norms that constrain how they interact. Systems reliably produce the results they are designed to produce, whether or not those results were ever consciously chosen.

Think About It

Before reading on, bring to mind one problem in your ministry that you have addressed at least twice. What did you do each time? What came back? Without yet having a name for it, where do you suspect the real source of that recurring problem actually lives?

The Systems Iceberg: A Map to What Is Hidden

The most useful conceptual tool for this kind of seeing is the Systems Iceberg, adapted for organizational contexts by Daniel Kim and Peter Senge. The iceberg is an apt metaphor: approximately 90% of an iceberg's mass lies below the waterline, invisible to observers on the surface. The same is true of the forces driving organizational behavior. The visible 10% — what we observe day to day — is shaped and sustained by a vast, largely unseen substructure (Senge, 1990; Gharajedaghi, 2011).

The iceberg has five levels, each deeper and more powerful than the one above it:

Level 1 — Events

Events are what we observe on the surface: the things that happen to us, the crises we respond to, the anomalies we notice. In the JYO case, the events are unmistakable — a $10,000 budget deficit, declining commitment numbers, exhausted volunteers, community opposition, and a founding leader announcing his resignation from the organizing team. Events demand our attention. They arrive with urgency and emotional charge. And because they are visible, they are where most leaders spend most of their energy.

The problem with reacting exclusively at the events level is not that events are unimportant; it is that they are uninformative about causation. An event tells you that something has gone wrong. It rarely tells you why (Senge, 1990).

Level 2 — Patterns

When we zoom out from a single event and ask whether it is part of a recurring trend, we move to the patterns level. Patterns reveal that what looked like an isolated event is actually a predictable consequence of a system doing exactly what it was set up to do. In the JYO case, a patterns-level observation would notice that commitments plateaued in Year Two and never recovered, that budget overruns increased in each of Years Three and Four, that community opposition grew in direct proportion to event scale, and that paid staff time shifted progressively away from discipleship toward event production. None of these is a one-time occurrence. Each is a trend — a system behaving consistently over time.

Recognizing patterns is an essential act of leadership humility. It requires acknowledging that the current situation is not simply bad luck or a one-off failure — it is a revelation about how the organization is structured (Senge et al., 1999).

Level 3 — Structures

Below patterns lie the structures that produce them: the policies, norms, relationships, physical arrangements, incentive systems, and organizational habits that are actually driving behavior. Structures are not purely formal; many of the most powerful are informal — the unwritten rules that govern how meetings run, how credit is distributed, how disagreement is managed, how success is measured.

In the JYO case, key structural elements include: the decision to hire paid staff for event production rather than volunteer development; the metric of attendance rather than discipleship depth as the primary measure of success; the organizational momentum that came from a growing budget and growing crowd; and the gradual inclusion of suburban churches whose community development commitments differed from the founding urban partners. These structural forces did not announce themselves. They accumulated quietly, each year's decision building on the last, until the structure of the organization bore no resemblance to the structure of its founding vision (Gharajedaghi, 2011).

Level 4 — Mental Models

Structures do not emerge from nowhere. They are built and sustained by mental models — the deeply held beliefs, assumptions, and values that shape how leaders see the world and what they regard as possible, desirable, or even thinkable. The mental model concept originates in cognitive science, where Philip Johnson-Laird demonstrated that human reasoning is mediated by internal representations — simplified maps of the world that we use to make sense of experience and predict outcomes (Johnson-Laird, 1983).

In organizational contexts, Senge argues that mental models are the invisible governors of structure: "Mental models are deeply ingrained assumptions, generalizations, or even pictures or images that influence how we understand the world and how we take action" (Senge, 1990, p. 8). The mental models driving the JYO's structural drift likely included beliefs such as: bigger crowds equal more Kingdom impact; community visibility legitimates ministry effectiveness; entertainment is a valid bridge to evangelism; growth without limit is inherently good. None of these beliefs was necessarily wrong in isolation. But operating unchallenged and unexamined, they silently authorized every structural decision that moved the JYO away from its missional core.

Level 5 — Intent

At the deepest level of the iceberg lies intent — the foundational purpose or goal that originally shaped the mental models and that continues (or fails to continue) to animate everything above it. Intent is not mission statement language. It is the lived, deeply personal conviction that drew the founders to this work in the first place — what Senge calls the "personal vision" that, when shared, becomes an organization's animating energy (Senge, 1990).

The JYO's founding intent was specific and powerful: to reach teenage boys who would never enter a church through a community-rooted, relationships-based outreach. David Williams proposed it not as a production but as a ministry of presence — coaches mentoring youth, volunteers coming alongside team members, a modest Saturday event with an evangelistic message. That intent was not abandoned overnight. It was eroded slowly by the weight of success metrics that pointed in a different direction. When we examine the JYO at the end of Year Four and ask, "What is this organization actually trying to accomplish?", the honest answer bears little resemblance to the founding intent. This gap between founding purpose and operational reality is what scholars of organizational learning call structural drift (Senge et al., 1999).

Capacity Limits: The Forces Working Against You

One of the most practically useful concepts in systems thinking is what the DVULI curriculum identifies as capacity limits — the forces or actions within a system that contribute to undesired results, often despite the best intentions of participants. Meadows describes these as the constraints built into a system's structure that prevent it from achieving the outcomes its participants desire (Meadows, 2008).

Capacity limits are particularly treacherous because they are invisible from inside the system. The JYO's organizing team never decided to become an entertainment production company. What they did, repeatedly and reasonably, was respond to the visible pressures in front of them: declining commitments in Year Three prompted them to book better entertainment; budget shortfalls prompted larger fundraising targets; community criticism prompted more police presence rather than a reconsideration of scale. Each response made local sense. Each response also reinforced the structural forces that were producing the very problems being addressed — a classic fixes that fail archetype (Senge, 1990).

Meadows identified twelve leverage points for intervening in a system, ranked by their potential effectiveness (Meadows, 1999). Her key finding was counterintuitive: the interventions leaders most commonly attempt — adjusting numbers, tweaking policies, reorganizing structures — are among the least effective. The most powerful leverage points involve changing the goals of the system (Level 5 in the Iceberg), the paradigms or mental models out of which the system arises (Level 4), and the power to change those paradigms themselves. As Meadows wrote, "The higher the leverage point, the more the system will resist changing it" (Meadows, 1999, p. 17). This explains why JYO's annual tactical adjustments never produced systemic change: they were operating at the lowest-leverage levels of the system.

"The primary task of systems leadership is not to manage events but to design the systems and structures that govern organizational behavior." — Senge (1990, p. 340, paraphrased)

For ministry leaders, this reorientation toward high-leverage intervention has a deeply biblical resonance. The DVULI Core Value of Leverage — the capacity for seemingly small actions to have disproportionately large effects — is most fully realized when those actions target the structural and mental-model levels of the iceberg rather than the event level. Nehemiah understood this intuitively: before he touched a single stone in Jerusalem's walls, he mapped the political, social, and resource systems he would need to engage. He worked at the level of structure and relationship, not crisis management (Boa, 2005, as cited in DVULI NC2 Pre-Assignment, 2024).

Widget 3: Six Thinking Hats Ministry Scenario

Before engaging the After-Action Review framework, this next tool develops the foundational capacity the AAR requires: the ability to deliberately expand your range of perspective. Edward de Bono's Six Thinking Hats methodology (introduced in the DVULI NC2 Workbook, 2024) provides a structured protocol for rotating through six distinct modes of thinking about a complex situation. Each hat mandates a different cognitive posture — no one hat is superior to another, but most leaders and most teams operate primarily from one or two, leaving critical analytical terrain unmapped.

The scenario below is drawn from the kinds of decisions DVULI participants regularly face: whether to partner with a secular community organization on a youth program that reaches significantly more youth but requires compromise on explicit faith content. Work through each hat deliberately and sequentially. Notice which hats feel natural and which create discomfort — that discomfort is a diagnostic signal about your default thinking style.

🎩 Six Thinking Hats Ministry Scenario
Scenario: Your ministry has been invited to co-lead a city-wide youth mentoring program with a well-resourced secular nonprofit. The partnership would triple your reach. The secular partner requires that explicitly faith-based content be kept to optional afterschool sessions rather than embedded in the main program curriculum. Work through each hat deliberately.

The After-Action Review: Collective Learning in Practice

Systems thinking without structured collective learning remains an intellectual exercise. The After-Action Review (AAR) is the practical bridge between systemic insight and organizational change. Developed by the United States Army Research Institute as a systematic method for military units to debrief after operations, the AAR creates a structured space in which teams can examine what they intended to accomplish, what actually happened, why the gap exists, and how to move forward together (Weick & Roberts, 1993).

Senge and colleagues adapted the AAR framework for organizational learning contexts in The Dance of Change, arguing that it addresses one of the primary obstacles to sustained organizational transformation: the failure to institutionalize learning from experience (Senge et al., 1999). Without a disciplined process for collective reflection, organizations repeat not just their successes but their failure modes — the same capacity limits reassert themselves, the same mental models redirect the next initiative, the same structural drift resumes its quiet work.

The AAR has six steps, each designed to move the team through a complete learning cycle:

  1. Intent — What did we set out to do, and why? This step establishes the original purpose as the reference point for all subsequent evaluation. It requires honesty about founding intent rather than retrospective reframing.
  2. Actual Results — What really happened? Both positive and negative outcomes must be named specifically, grounded in evidence rather than impression. This step often surfaces information that circulates informally but has never been spoken aloud collectively.
  3. Gap Analysis — Why did our actual results differ from our intent? This is the heart of the AAR and the point at which systems thinking becomes essential. The gap between intent and result is almost never explained by the immediate preceding actions; it is almost always explained by structural and mental-model level forces that accumulated over time.
  4. Lessons Learned — What generalized insights can we extract? Lessons that remain specific to a single incident have limited value; the goal is to identify transferable principles about how this system behaves under these conditions.
  5. Shared Vision — How can we move forward together? This step re-grounds the team in a common commitment to the future, preventing the AAR from becoming a blame exercise. It reconnects the group to the founding intent that first animated their work.
  6. Next Steps — What must we do, and by when? Without specific commitments, even the most insightful AAR dissolves into good intentions. Next steps must be assigned, dated, and followed up.

Applying the AAR framework to the JYO case at the DVULI Second National Conference (DVULI NC2 Workbook, 2024) revealed something important: nearly every stakeholder group had pieces of the analysis that no other group possessed. Urban pastors understood the community relationships that had been fractured; event staff could see the operational pressures that had redirected resources; founding volunteers articulated the spiritual vision that had been crowded out; suburban church representatives named the demographic assumptions that had shaped programming choices. No individual perspective contained the whole picture. The AAR, at its best, creates the conditions for that whole picture to emerge collectively.

Think About It

Think of a significant ministry initiative from the past two years. Could you articulate, clearly and honestly, all six steps of an AAR for that initiative right now — especially the Gap Analysis? What would you need to know or gather to do so? Who would need to be in the room for the full picture to be visible?

Widget 2: After-Action Review Builder

The tool below guides you through a complete six-step After-Action Review. You may work with the JYO Basketball Tournament case or substitute a real situation from your own ministry context. Each step includes an anchoring prompt and a reminder to ground your responses in specific evidence rather than general impressions. When all six panels are complete, generate your compiled AAR for use with your real ministry team.

📋 After-Action Review Builder
Complete all six steps, then generate your compiled AAR guide — ready to use with your actual ministry team.

From Insight to Intervention: Designing at the Right Level

Armed with the Iceberg, the AAR, and the expanded perspective of the Six Hats, the ministry leader faces a final and critical question: at what level do I now intervene? Senge's answer, stated plainly in "The Leader's New Work," is that the primary work of leaders in learning organizations is not to manage events but to design the systems and structures that shape organizational behavior (Senge, 1990b). He distinguishes between event-level leadership — reactive crisis management — and systemic leadership — the deliberate design of enabling structures.

For the JYO, a systemic intervention would not focus primarily on booking a better speaker for Year Five or restructuring the budget line items. It would ask structural and mental-model questions: What would it mean to redesign the tournament's success metrics to center discipleship depth rather than attendance? What norms would need to change to make that measurement visible and valued? What mental models about ministry effectiveness are being reinforced by the current structure, and how might we surface and examine them collectively?

This is exactly the territory that the After-Action Review, applied rigorously and honestly, is designed to map. When Pastor Jones proposed a community dialogue as the response to the Year Four crisis, he was — intuitively — creating the conditions for a collective AAR. The question was whether the dialogue would remain at the events and patterns levels ("attendance is down, let's fix the program") or would dive into the structural and mental-model levels where actual transformation becomes possible.

Gharajedaghi emphasizes that systems thinking is not primarily an analytical technique but a design discipline — a way of seeing that enables more intentional intervention (Gharajedaghi, 2011). The Iceberg model, the AAR, and the Six Hats are not ends in themselves; they are tools for generating the quality of insight that makes structural redesign possible rather than merely reactive patching. Together, they form the systems leadership toolkit that the DVULI Leverage Core Value points toward: the disciplined capacity to find the high-leverage places in the ministry system where small, intentional changes produce large, sustainable transformation.

Systems Thinking as a Spiritual Discipline

It is worth pausing to note the profound spiritual resonance of what systems thinking asks of us as leaders. The biblical invitation to be transformed by the renewing of your mind (Romans 12:2, NIV) is, at its core, an invitation to examine and revise the mental models — the deeply held assumptions and worldviews — through which we see ourselves, others, and the world. The DVULI NC2 Pre-Assignment (2024) draws directly on the tribe of Issachar as a scriptural archetype for systems leadership: men who "understood the times and knew what Israel should do" (1 Chronicles 12:32, NIV). To understand the times is to see below the surface of events, to recognize patterns, to identify the structural forces shaping the moment, and to act with wisdom rather than mere urgency.

Nehemiah's leadership through the rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls — examined across DVULI in both the scenario planning and systems thinking modules — is a masterclass in iceberg-level thinking applied in real time. He did not simply gather stones. He mapped the political systems, secured structural permissions, built relational coalitions, and attended to the mental models of both allies and opponents before any visible construction began (Boa, 2005). The wall was built not by reacting to opposition but by designing a system of response, community, and accountable leadership that could sustain itself through opposition.

For urban ministry leaders operating in communities shaped by generations of structural injustice and systemic neglect, this kind of deep-level seeing is not merely professionally advantageous — it is an act of love. Heather Gordon's observation in the JYO dialogue — "When did this become all about the big show?" — was not a program critique. It was a lament for a community whose real needs were being obscured by metrics that measured crowd size rather than transformed lives. Systems thinking restores the clarity to see those real needs, and the analytical tools to design interventions that genuinely address them.

Key Takeaways

  • Most recurring organizational problems originate not in events but in the deeper structural and mental-model levels of the Systems Iceberg — reacting at the event level guarantees the problem will return.
  • The five levels of the Systems Iceberg (Events, Patterns, Structures, Mental Models, Intent) provide a navigational map for locating the real source of organizational behavior — and the highest-leverage point for intervention.
  • Capacity limits are the structural forces within a system that consistently produce undesired results despite good intentions; naming them is the first step to addressing them.
  • Meadows' leverage point framework confirms that the interventions leaders most commonly attempt (adjusting numbers, tweaking policies) are among the least effective; changing mental models and system goals produces the most durable transformation.
  • The After-Action Review creates a structured, six-step process for collective learning that prevents organizations from repeating failure modes — but only if it is applied honestly at all five iceberg levels, not just the events surface.
  • The Six Thinking Hats protocol is a practical tool for expanding beyond default thinking styles, ensuring that complex decisions are examined from data, emotional, creative, cautionary, optimistic, and process-management perspectives simultaneously.
  • Systems leadership — designing the structures that shape behavior rather than managing the events those structures produce — is the highest form of the DVULI Core Value of Leverage in organizational practice.
  • The biblical invitation to be transformed by the renewing of the mind is, at its core, an invitation to examine and revise the mental models that govern how we see and act in the world.

Looking Ahead

Session 14 turns from systems insight to organizational alignment — examining how the structures and strategies of your ministry can be redesigned to embody the vision you have been developing throughout DVULI. You will apply the Breakthrough Planning framework to the structural redesign questions this session has surfaced, moving from iceberg analysis to concrete, accountable action steps that target the high-leverage levels of your particular ministry system. Bring your completed AAR and your iceberg summary — they will form the foundation of your next breakthrough conversation.

blocks.title: Key Concepts
concepts: Systems Iceberg model: Events, Patterns, Structures, Mental Models, and Intent as five levels of systemic understanding
The After-Action Review (AAR): Intent, Actual Results, Gap Analysis, Lessons Learned, Shared Vision, Next Steps
Capacity limits: the forces or actions within a system that contribute to undesired results
Leverage points in systems: where to intervene for maximum change with minimum effort

items.question: In the Systems Iceberg model, which level represents the visible, surface-level occurrences that leaders most commonly react to?
items.correctFeedback: Events sit at the top of the iceberg — the visible tip above the waterline. They are the specific, observable occurrences (a budget overrun, a volunteer quitting, a conflict) that leaders notice and typically respond to first, often without examining what lies beneath them.

distractors.feedback: Students may confuse Patterns with Events because both are relatively observable. However, Patterns emerge only when a leader notices that similar events recur over time — they require looking beyond a single occurrence to see trends and consistencies.
Structures are below the waterline and include habits, norms, policies, and relationships that drive recurring patterns. They are not directly visible on the surface the way Events are.
Mental Models are among the deepest levels of the iceberg, consisting of deeply held beliefs and assumptions. They are far from the surface-level occurrences that leaders typically react to first.

items.question: According to the chapter, what is the primary reason that problems often reappear in different forms after a leader believes they have been solved?
items.correctAnswer: The leader addressed surface events without changing the underlying system that produced them
items.correctFeedback: The chapter's central argument is that managing events without seeing the underlying system — the structures, mental models, and intent below the waterline — leads to recurring problems. Genuine breakthrough requires intervening at deeper levels of the iceberg.

distractors.text: The leader lacked the authority to implement lasting organizational change
distractors.feedback: While authority can be a real constraint, the chapter frames the recurring-problem phenomenon as a perceptual issue — an inability to see systemic causes — rather than primarily a power or authority deficit.

distractors.text: The leader failed to communicate the solution clearly enough to the team
distractors.feedback: Communication failures are a common leadership concern, but the chapter specifically attributes recurring problems to event-level reactions that leave deeper systemic structures intact, not to poor communication of otherwise sound solutions.

distractors.text: The leader did not act quickly enough before the problem became entrenched
distractors.feedback: Speed of response addresses events even more aggressively at the surface level. The chapter argues that faster event-level action does not solve the problem; deeper systemic understanding is what prevents recurrence.

items.question: In the Systems Iceberg model, which level includes the habits, norms, policies, and relationships that drive recurring patterns of behavior?
items.correctFeedback: Structures are the organizational and relational arrangements — policies, habits, norms, incentive systems, and relationship dynamics — that sit below the waterline and actively produce the patterns that leaders observe on the surface.

distractors.feedback: Patterns describe the trends and recurring themes that emerge from multiple events over time. They reveal consistency, but they are the result of structures rather than the driving force behind them.
Mental Models are one level deeper than Structures. They are the beliefs and assumptions that create and sustain structures, but they are not the habits, norms, and policies themselves.

Intent is the deepest level of the iceberg — the foundational purpose or goal that shapes mental models. It does not refer to the concrete organizational arrangements like policies, norms, and habits.

items.question: Which sequence correctly orders the five levels of the Systems Iceberg from the most visible (surface) to the deepest?
items.correctAnswer: Events, Patterns, Structures, Mental Models, Intent
items.correctFeedback: The iceberg moves from the observable surface downward: Events are what we see, Patterns emerge from recurring events, Structures are the organizational forces producing those patterns, Mental Models are the deep beliefs sustaining those structures, and Intent is the foundational purpose shaping everything above it.

distractors.text: Events, Structures, Patterns, Intent, Mental Models
distractors.feedback: This sequence incorrectly places Structures before Patterns. In the model, Patterns are recognized before one can identify the Structures that produce them. It also reverses Intent and Mental Models at the deepest levels.

distractors.text: Intent, Mental Models, Structures, Patterns, Events
distractors.feedback: This sequence is actually the correct order from deepest to most visible — the reverse of what the question asks. Students who think 'bottom-up' rather than 'surface-down' as the question specifies may select this.

distractors.text: Events, Patterns, Mental Models, Structures, Intent
distractors.feedback: This sequence incorrectly places Mental Models before Structures. In the Iceberg model, Structures (policies, norms, habits) sit between Patterns and Mental Models because structures are more visible than the deep beliefs that sustain them.

items.question: In the After-Action Review (AAR) framework presented in the chapter, what is the purpose of the 'Gap Analysis' step?
items.correctAnswer: To examine the difference between intended outcomes and actual results and explore why that difference occurred
items.correctFeedback: Gap Analysis is the diagnostic heart of the AAR. By placing intended outcomes alongside actual results, teams can identify discrepancies and investigate the systemic causes — moving beyond blame to structural understanding of why outcomes differed from plans.

distractors.text: To identify the individual team members responsible for shortfalls in performance
distractors.feedback: This reflects a blame-oriented approach that the AAR is specifically designed to avoid. Gap Analysis examines systemic causes of the difference between intent and results, not individual fault.

distractors.text: To evaluate whether the original vision was ambitious enough to inspire the team
distractors.feedback: While Shared Vision is a component of the AAR, Gap Analysis is not about evaluating the quality or ambition of the vision. It focuses on understanding why actual results differed from what was intended.

distractors.text: To determine the financial cost of missed targets so the budget can be adjusted
distractors.feedback: Financial analysis might be one element within a gap review, but the AAR's Gap Analysis is a broader systemic inquiry into why outcomes diverged from intentions — it is not limited to or primarily focused on budgetary concerns.

items.question: In the Jackson Youth Outreach case study, the 3-on-3 Basketball Tournament gradually drifted from its original purpose. Which level of the Systems Iceberg best describes this shift from community development to entertainment production?
items.correctFeedback: The drift from community development to entertainment production represents a structural change — the organizational habits, priorities, partnerships, and resource allocations gradually shifted in ways that redirected the event's focus. This structural drift is what produced the observable patterns of declining commitments, budget overruns, and community opposition.

distractors.feedback: The declining commitments, budget overruns, and community opposition are the events — the visible symptoms. The drift from community development to entertainment production is the deeper structural shift that caused those events, not an event itself.

Patterns would describe the recurring trends that emerged (e.g., year-over-year budget increases, consistent volunteer attrition). The fundamental organizational shift from one mission orientation to another is a structural change that produced those patterns.

Intent is the deepest foundational purpose. The case study suggests the original intent (community development) may have remained unchanged or unchallenged even as structures drifted — which is precisely why the drift was so dangerous and hard to see.

items.question: A church implements a new volunteer scheduling system to solve chronic understaffing at Sunday services. Six months later, understaffing persists because longtime volunteers feel micromanaged and have quietly stepped back. A systems thinker would most likely conclude that the intervention failed because it targeted which level of the iceberg?
items.correctFeedback: The scheduling system addressed the visible symptom (empty volunteer slots) at the event level without examining the deeper structural and relational dynamics — such as volunteer autonomy, trust, and relational norms — that actually drive participation. This is the classic error the chapter warns against: reacting to surface events while the substructure remains unaddressed.

distractors.feedback: If the intervention had targeted mental models, it would have addressed deep beliefs about volunteerism, trust, and control. The scheduling system did the opposite — it was a surface-level operational fix that ignored those deeper beliefs.

Targeting intent would mean re-examining the church's foundational purpose regarding community and service. A new scheduling tool is far from an intent-level intervention; it is a tactical, event-level response.

While a scheduling system is technically a structural change, the chapter distinguishes between superficial procedural adjustments and genuine structural interventions. The system addressed the symptom (unfilled slots) rather than the relational structures and norms that actually governed volunteer engagement.

items.question: The concept of 'capacity limits' in systems thinking refers to which of the following?
items.correctAnswer: Forces or actions within a system that contribute to undesired results
items.correctFeedback: Capacity limits are the internal systemic forces — such as resource constraints, overextended personnel, competing priorities, or structural bottlenecks — that push back against growth or change and contribute to undesired outcomes. They explain why well-intentioned efforts often produce diminishing or counterproductive results.

distractors.text: The maximum number of programs an organization can operate simultaneously
distractors.feedback: While program overload might be one example of hitting a capacity limit, the concept is much broader. Capacity limits encompass any systemic forces or actions that contribute to undesired results, not just a numerical count of programs.

distractors.text: The point at which a leader's personal energy and skill can no longer sustain organizational growth
distractors.feedback: This describes individual burnout, which could be one manifestation of a capacity limit. However, the concept refers more broadly to systemic forces — not just a single leader's limitations — that produce undesired results within the system.

distractors.text: External constraints imposed on an organization by its funding sources or regulatory environment
distractors.feedback: External constraints can certainly limit an organization, but capacity limits as defined in the chapter refer specifically to forces or actions within the system. The concept focuses on internal dynamics, not externally imposed restrictions.

items.question: A ministry leader discovers that her organization's unspoken belief — 'growth means God's blessing' — has driven the staff to scale programs beyond their ability to maintain relational depth with participants. According to the Systems Iceberg model, this discovery represents insight at which level?
items.correctFeedback: The belief 'growth means God's blessing' is a deeply held assumption that shapes how the organization structures its programs and measures success. Mental Models are exactly these kinds of beliefs — often unspoken and unexamined — that create and sustain the structures which in turn produce observable patterns and events.

distractors.text: Patterns of program expansion and staff burnout that repeat across multiple ministry cycles
distractors.feedback: The pattern would be the observable trend of continual program expansion at the expense of relational depth. The underlying belief driving that expansion is a mental model, which sits deeper than the pattern it produces.

The structures would include the staffing models, program designs, and metrics that prioritize growth. The belief that 'growth means God's blessing' is the mental model that creates and sustains those structures — it operates at a deeper level.

Intent is the foundational purpose (e.g., 'to develop disciples in community'). The belief about growth equaling blessing is an assumption about how to fulfill that purpose — a mental model — rather than the foundational purpose itself.

items.question: A systems thinker analyzing a struggling after-school program identifies that changing one specific policy — requiring all mentors to live within the neighborhood they serve — would likely shift volunteer recruitment patterns, reshape community trust, and realign the program with its original mission. This policy change is best described as which systems thinking concept?
items.correctFeedback: A leverage point is a place in a system where a relatively small intervention can produce disproportionately large, cascading effects throughout the system. The residency requirement is a single structural change that would influence multiple system dynamics — recruitment, trust, and mission alignment — making it a classic example of high-leverage intervention.

distractors.feedback: Capacity limits are forces within a system that contribute to undesired results. While the current lack of neighborhood-based mentors might reflect a capacity limit, the proposed policy change itself is not a capacity limit — it is an intervention designed to address systemic problems at a high-leverage point.

An event-level response would address a single visible symptom without affecting deeper dynamics. This policy change is structural and has cascading effects across multiple system dimensions, making it far more than an event-level reaction.

Gap analysis is a step in the After-Action Review that examines the difference between intended and actual outcomes. Identifying a strategic policy intervention is not a gap analysis — it is the application of systems thinking to find the most effective point of intervention.

questions.stem: In the Systems Iceberg model, which level represents the deeply held beliefs and assumptions that create and sustain an organization's structures?
options: Mental Models
Patterns
Structures
Events

feedback.correct: Mental Models sit near the base of the iceberg and represent the deeply held beliefs and assumptions that shape and sustain the structures above them. They are largely invisible and often unconscious, which is why they are so powerful and so difficult to change.
feedback.incorrect: Many students confuse the iceberg levels. Remember the order from surface to depth: Events → Patterns → Structures → Mental Models → Intent. Mental Models are the deeply held beliefs and assumptions — not the observable habits or recurring trends.

variants.stem: Which level of the Systems Iceberg sits at the very foundation, representing the purpose or goal that ultimately shapes all the levels above it?
options: Intent
Mental Models
Structures
Patterns

feedback.correct: Intent is the deepest level of the iceberg — the foundational purpose or goal that shapes the mental models, which in turn create structures, which produce patterns, which generate observable events. When intent drifts, everything above it eventually shifts as well.
feedback.incorrect: Intent is the deepest, most foundational level of the iceberg — not Mental Models (which sit one level above Intent) and not Structures or Patterns (which are closer to the surface). Think of it as the bedrock beneath everything else.

questions.stem: The After-Action Review (AAR) framework adapted by Senge for community ministry includes six steps. Which of the following is the correct sequence for the first three steps?
options: Intent → Actual Results → Gap Analysis
Actual Results → Intent → Lessons Learned
Gap Analysis → Lessons Learned → Shared Vision
Shared Vision → Intent → Next Steps

feedback.correct: The AAR begins by clarifying what was intended, then examining what actually happened, and then analyzing the gap between the two. This sequence ensures the team measures results against purpose before drawing lessons or planning ahead.
feedback.incorrect: The AAR follows a deliberate logic: you must first know what you intended, then what actually resulted, before you can analyze the gap. The full sequence is Intent → Actual Results → Gap Analysis → Lessons Learned → Shared Vision → Next Steps.

variants.stem: After a ministry team identifies lessons learned during an After-Action Review, what are the final two steps of the six-step AAR framework?
options: Shared Vision → Next Steps
Gap Analysis → Next Steps
Next Steps → Shared Vision
Intent → Actual Results

feedback.correct: After Lessons Learned, the AAR moves into forward-looking steps: establishing a Shared Vision for the future and then defining concrete Next Steps. This transition from backward-looking analysis to forward-looking commitment is what makes the AAR a tool for collective learning, not just blame assignment.
feedback.incorrect: The AAR's final two steps are explicitly forward-looking. After examining what happened and what was learned (steps 1-4), the team builds Shared Vision and then identifies Next Steps. The order matters: vision before action ensures alignment.

questions.stem: In the Jackson Youth Outreach case study, the 3-on-3 Basketball Tournament gradually evolved from a focused community-rooted initiative into a sprawling entertainment event. At which level of the Systems Iceberg does this organizational drift primarily operate?
options: Structures — the habits, norms, policies, and relationships that shifted over time
Events — the specific incidents of budget overruns and declining commitments
Patterns — the recurring trend of growing larger each year
Intent — the foundational purpose was deliberately changed by leaders
justifications: The drift involved gradual, often invisible changes in decision-making processes, resource allocation norms, and partnership relationships that accumulated into a fundamentally different organizational structure
Each individual budget overrun and scheduling conflict was a discrete event that independently caused the organization to lose its mission
The pattern of annual growth is the root cause because patterns always determine organizational direction regardless of deeper dynamics
Leaders consciously decided to shift from community development to entertainment production, making this an intentional strategic pivot

feedback.correct: The Jackson case illustrates structural drift — the gradual, often invisible accumulation of changes in habits, norms, decision-making processes, and resource allocation that transform an organization's character without anyone explicitly deciding to change direction. The events (budget overruns, opposition) are symptoms; the patterns (annual escalation) are observable trends; but the structures are where the real drift occurred.
feedback.incorrect: Events like budget overruns are surface symptoms, not the level where drift operates. Patterns like annual growth describe what kept happening, but don't explain why. And the leaders never deliberately changed their intent — the drift happened below conscious awareness. Structural change — in habits, norms, policies, and relationships — is where mission drift takes root.
feedback.wrongReason: Students often correctly identify the structural level but then describe it in terms of conscious decisions or surface events. The key insight is that structural drift is gradual and largely invisible — it's the accumulation of many small changes in how things are done, not a single deliberate choice.

variants.stem: A community health ministry starts by training neighborhood residents as peer health advocates. Over five years, it gradually shifts to hiring professional staff who deliver services to passive recipients. Using the Systems Iceberg, at which level is the core problem?
options: Structures — the hiring practices, funding requirements, and operational norms shifted the ministry's character
Events — specific staff hires caused the problem
Intent — the board formally voted to abandon resident empowerment
Patterns — the trend of professionalization is itself the root cause
justifications: Structural elements like hiring policies, funder reporting requirements, and professional norms gradually displaced the original empowerment approach without any single decision point
Each individual hiring decision was the direct cause of the ministry's transformation, independent of any deeper organizational dynamics
The board's conscious decision to professionalize proves that intent is always the level where organizational change originates
Patterns of professionalization operate independently of organizational structures and cause change through sheer repetition

feedback.correct: Like the Jackson case, this scenario illustrates structural drift. The hiring practices, funding requirements, and professional norms — all structural elements — gradually displaced the original empowerment model. No one decided to abandon resident empowerment; the structures evolved to make professionalization the path of least resistance.
feedback.incorrect: Individual hires are events. The trend toward professionalization is a pattern. But neither explains why the shift happened. The answer lies in the structural level — where policies, norms, and operational habits quietly reshaped what the ministry could and would do.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may correctly identify the structural level but attribute it to conscious decision-making. The critical insight is that structural drift is usually unconscious — it happens through the accumulated weight of many small operational choices, not through deliberate strategic pivots.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: Leaders who respond only to events will find that problems reappear in different forms over time.
questions.reason: Events are surface manifestations produced by deeper patterns, structures, and mental models that remain unchanged when only events are addressed.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason directly explains the assertion. This is the core argument of Systems Thinking: events are symptoms generated by the iceberg's deeper levels. Addressing symptoms without changing the underlying structures and mental models guarantees that the system will produce new versions of the same problems.
feedback.incorrect: A common error is to see these as independently true rather than causally connected. The reason doesn't merely happen to be true alongside the assertion — it directly explains why event-level responses fail. The iceberg's deep structure generates events, so leaving that structure untouched means the system keeps producing problematic events.

variants.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
variants.assertion: The After-Action Review is effective because it assigns blame for failures to specific individuals.
variants.reason: Accountability for results requires identifying which person made the wrong decision.

feedback.correct: Both statements are false. The AAR is explicitly designed as a collective learning process, not a blame-assignment mechanism. Its effectiveness comes from examining systemic gaps between intent and results, not from identifying individual culprits. True accountability in systems thinking means understanding how structures and mental models — not just individual choices — produce outcomes.
feedback.incorrect: The AAR is not a blame-assignment tool — it's a collective learning process. Its six steps (Intent, Actual Results, Gap Analysis, Lessons Learned, Shared Vision, Next Steps) are designed to help teams understand systemic dynamics, not to find someone to punish. Both statements fundamentally misrepresent the AAR's purpose.

questions.stem: A church's youth ministry consistently loses volunteer leaders after 6-9 months. Each time a volunteer leaves, the pastor personally recruits a replacement. After three years, the pastor is exhausted and the problem persists. At which iceberg level is the pastor's response operating?
options: Events — reacting to each individual departure as it occurs
Patterns — recognizing the recurring 6-9 month cycle
Structures — changing the volunteer training and support systems
Mental Models — questioning assumptions about what volunteers need

feedback.correct: The pastor is operating at the Events level — treating each volunteer departure as an isolated incident requiring a one-time fix (recruit a replacement). Despite three years of evidence, the response never moves below the surface to ask why volunteers consistently leave at 6-9 months. This is the classic trap of event-level leadership: constant firefighting that never addresses root causes.
feedback.incorrect: Recognizing that the problem recurs would be Pattern-level awareness, but the pastor's response (recruit a replacement each time) shows event-level reaction, not pattern-level analysis. A structural response would redesign the volunteer experience; a mental model response would question fundamental assumptions about volunteerism. The pastor is doing none of these — just replacing each lost volunteer.

variants.stem: A nonprofit director notices that their annual fundraising gala raises less money each year. Each year, the director responds by changing the catering company and hiring a more expensive entertainer. After four years, costs have risen and revenue has continued to decline. At which iceberg level is the director operating?
options: Events — treating each year's disappointing result as an isolated problem to fix with surface-level changes
Patterns — analyzing the multi-year decline to identify trends
Structures — redesigning the entire fundraising strategy and donor relationships
Mental Models — questioning whether galas are the right fundraising approach

feedback.correct: The director is stuck at the Events level — responding to each year's disappointing gala as if better catering or entertainment will solve the problem. Despite four years of declining returns, the response never dives deeper to examine why donor engagement is falling. Changing surface-level event details (caterer, entertainer) while leaving the underlying fundraising structure and mental models intact is classic event-level reaction.
feedback.incorrect: Pattern-level awareness would involve analyzing the multi-year trend. Structural intervention would redesign the fundraising approach. Mental model questioning would challenge whether galas are effective at all. The director's repeated surface-level tweaks (new caterer, better entertainer) represent pure event-level reaction — addressing symptoms without examining causes.

questions.stem: Classify each statement about the Systems Iceberg model and systems thinking as 'Always true,' 'Sometimes true,' or 'Never true':

statements.text: A single incident of budget overrun in a ministry program is an example of an Event in the iceberg model
Mental models are consciously held and easily articulated by the leaders who hold them
Intervening at the Events level produces lasting systemic change
Structures include informal norms and relationship dynamics, not just formal policies
The After-Action Review can surface dynamics at all five levels of the iceberg
Identifying a leverage point guarantees that change will be easy and unresisted
Capacity limits within a system always result from insufficient funding

feedback.correct: The key distinctions here involve boundary cases. Mental models are sometimes conscious but are often deeply unconscious — that's what makes them so powerful and hard to change. The AAR can reach all five levels, but only if facilitators intentionally probe below events and patterns. Leverage points identify where to intervene efficiently, but they don't guarantee ease — systemic resistance is common. And capacity limits can stem from many forces (norms, relationships, beliefs, policies), not just funding.
feedback.incorrect: The most common errors involve treating 'sometimes' statements as 'always' or 'never.' Mental models can be conscious, but they are often unconscious — making the answer 'sometimes,' not 'always.' The AAR has the potential to reach deep iceberg levels, but doesn't automatically do so. Also, watch for the trap of assuming that identifying a leverage point eliminates resistance — systems push back against change even at high-leverage intervention points.

variants.stem: Classify each statement about systems thinking in ministry contexts as 'Always true,' 'Sometimes true,' or 'Never true':

statements.text: Recurring volunteer turnover every 6-9 months represents a Pattern in the iceberg model
Changing a mental model automatically transforms all the structures built upon it
The deepest level of the iceberg — Intent — is shared and understood by all members of an organization
Leverage points are always found at the Mental Models level of the iceberg
An organization can experience structural drift without anyone making a deliberate decision to change direction
The AAR framework is designed primarily for assigning blame after failures
Capacity limits include forces like cultural norms and unspoken assumptions, not just tangible resource shortages

feedback.correct: Key boundary distinctions: Mental model changes don't automatically cascade upward because structures have their own inertia and reinforcing dynamics. Intent is sometimes shared but often assumed rather than explicitly articulated. Leverage points can exist at any iceberg level, though deeper interventions tend to be more powerful. Structural drift, by definition, happens without deliberate decisions — that's what makes it so dangerous.
feedback.incorrect: Watch for absolute thinking. Changing a mental model is powerful but doesn't automatically transform structures — structures resist change. Leverage points are most powerful at deeper levels but can exist at any level depending on the system. The AAR is a learning tool, never a blame tool. And structural drift is defined by its unconscious, gradual nature.

questions.stem: The Systems Iceberg model has five levels: Events, Patterns, Structures, Mental Models, and Intent. Only one level — Events — is visible above the waterline. What percentage of the model's total levels exist below the waterline, invisible to leaders who only respond to surface phenomena?

feedback.correct: Exactly 4 of the 5 iceberg levels (80%) exist below the waterline: Patterns, Structures, Mental Models, and Intent. This proportion dramatizes the central insight of systems thinking — the vast majority of what drives organizational behavior is invisible to leaders who only react to surface events.
feedback.incorrect: Students often underestimate how much of the iceberg is hidden. Only Events sit above the waterline. The other four levels — Patterns, Structures, Mental Models, and Intent — are all below the surface. That's 4 out of 5, or 80%. This is why event-level leadership is so insufficient: it addresses only 20% of the system.

variants.stem: The After-Action Review (AAR) framework has six steps: Intent, Actual Results, Gap Analysis, Lessons Learned, Shared Vision, and Next Steps. What percentage of the AAR's steps are retrospective — focused on examining what already happened and why — rather than prospective (focused on the future)?

feedback.correct: Four of six AAR steps (67%) are retrospective: clarifying Intent, examining Actual Results, conducting Gap Analysis, and extracting Lessons Learned. Only the final two steps — Shared Vision and Next Steps — are forward-looking. This 2:1 ratio reflects the AAR's core conviction that effective future action requires thorough understanding of the past.
feedback.incorrect: Count carefully: Intent (what we planned — retrospective), Actual Results (what happened — retrospective), Gap Analysis (why the difference — retrospective), Lessons Learned (what we now know — retrospective), Shared Vision (future), Next Steps (future). That's 4 retrospective out of 6 total, approximately 67%. The AAR deliberately weights understanding the past over planning the future.

questions.stem: A neighborhood after-school tutoring ministry has been running for five years. In the past eighteen months, the following has occurred: student attendance dropped 40%, two long-time volunteer tutors quit citing burnout, parents complained about inconsistent quality, the director responded by posting recruitment flyers and offering pizza parties to boost attendance, and a local funder threatened to withdraw support. The director is frustrated, saying, 'We keep putting out fires but nothing gets better.' Using the Systems Iceberg model, the concept of leverage points, and the After-Action Review framework, what should the ministry team do first?
options: Conduct an AAR to clarify original intent versus current reality, then use the iceberg to trace surface events down to the structural and mental model levels where leverage points can be identified
Double the recruitment budget and offer larger incentives because the events clearly indicate a marketing and motivation problem
Replace the director immediately because declining metrics over eighteen months indicate failed leadership at the Intent level
Survey parents about preferred pizza toppings and tutoring schedules because addressing stakeholder satisfaction is the highest leverage intervention
justifications: The AAR's structured process reveals the gap between original intent and current results, and the iceberg model then guides the team to look beneath events and patterns to the structures and mental models where small, well-placed interventions (leverage points) can produce systemic improvement
The recruitment and incentive approach is the highest-leverage intervention because events are the most actionable level of the iceberg and produce the fastest visible results
Replacing the director addresses the Intent level because all organizational intent flows through the leader, making leadership change the deepest possible structural intervention
Parent surveys represent mental model-level intervention because understanding stakeholder preferences reveals the deepest assumptions driving the system

feedback.correct: This question synthesizes three key concepts from the session. The director's complaint ('we keep putting out fires') is a classic symptom of event-level leadership. The AAR provides the structured process for the team to collectively examine intent vs. reality (steps 1-3), extract lessons (step 4), and build shared vision and next steps (steps 5-6). The iceberg model then provides the diagnostic framework to trace events (attendance drop, volunteer departures) down through patterns (18-month decline) to structures (inadequate volunteer support, program design issues) and mental models (assumptions about what tutoring should look like). Leverage points are found at these deeper levels — not in pizza parties or recruitment flyers.
feedback.incorrect: The recruitment-and-incentives approach is pure event-level reaction — exactly what the director has been doing with no success. Replacing the director confuses leadership with intent (intent is a systemic property, not a person). Parent preference surveys address surface satisfaction, not the deep structural and mental model dynamics producing the decline. The first step must be a systematic diagnostic process (AAR) combined with a depth framework (iceberg) to find where small changes could produce large systemic effects (leverage points).
feedback.wrongReason: Students who choose the correct answer but the wrong justification often understand that an AAR and iceberg analysis are needed but misidentify why. The key connection is that the AAR surfaces the intent-reality gap, the iceberg provides the framework for diving below events to deeper causes, and leverage points tell us where to intervene for maximum effect with minimum effort. All three concepts work together — not just one at a time.

variants.stem: A church-based job training program for young adults was launched three years ago with strong community support. Recently: completion rates dropped to 30%, two corporate partners withdrew, the pastor responded by adding a motivational speaker to each session, remaining participants report the curriculum feels irrelevant, and staff meetings focus entirely on next week's attendance numbers. A veteran staff member observes, 'We're so busy counting heads that we forgot why we started this.' Drawing on the Systems Iceberg, leverage points, and the AAR framework, what is the most effective first response?
options: Facilitate an AAR with the full team to recover original intent and analyze the gap, then use the iceberg to trace the attendance and partner problems to their structural and mental model roots where leverage points exist
Hire a better motivational speaker and increase social media advertising because the events indicate an engagement and awareness problem
Eliminate the program entirely because three years of decline proves the original intent was flawed from the beginning
Add more corporate partners to replace the ones who left, since funding is always the deepest leverage point in any system
justifications: The AAR recovers the intent-reality gap and builds shared understanding, while the iceberg guides the team below surface events and patterns to discover the structural dynamics and mental models where high-leverage, low-effort interventions can redirect the program
Better speakers and advertising address the pattern level of the iceberg, which is where leverage points are always most effective
Program elimination is the highest-leverage intervention because it addresses the Intent level by removing a flawed foundational purpose
Corporate partnerships are structural leverage points because funding structures always determine organizational mental models

feedback.correct: The veteran staffer's observation ('we forgot why we started this') points directly to intent drift — the deepest iceberg level. The AAR's first step — clarifying original intent — is precisely what's needed. From there, the team can examine actual results, analyze the gap, and use the iceberg to trace surface events (partner withdrawal, low completion) through patterns (three-year decline) to structures (curriculum design, partnership management) and mental models (assumptions about what job readiness means). Leverage points at these deeper levels will be far more effective than another motivational speaker.
feedback.incorrect: Adding speakers or advertising is event-level reaction. Eliminating the program confuses 'the intent drifted' with 'the intent was flawed.' Adding corporate partners treats a symptom (lost funding) as a root cause. The staff member's insight about forgetting original purpose signals that the team needs a structured process (AAR) to recover intent and a depth framework (iceberg) to find where the system went off course.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may correctly choose the AAR and iceberg approach but misunderstand the mechanism. The crucial point is that these three tools work together: the AAR provides process structure, the iceberg provides diagnostic depth, and leverage point thinking provides strategic focus. Getting the answer right requires understanding how all three concepts integrate.

questions.stem: In Week 8, Breakthrough was defined as change that meets three criteria. Which of the following correctly identifies all three?
options: Strategic, Significant, and Sustainable
Spiritual, Structural, and Systemic
Simple, Scalable, and Sustainable
Strategic, Spiritual, and Significant

feedback.correct: Breakthrough is defined as change that is Strategic (intentionally directed toward a clear goal), Significant (meaningful in scope and impact, not a minor tweak), and Sustainable (enduring over time, not a flash-in-the-pan improvement). All three criteria must be present for genuine breakthrough.
feedback.incorrect: The three S's of Breakthrough are Strategic, Significant, and Sustainable. This is a specific technical definition from the course — not a list of general positive adjectives. Spiritual and systemic qualities may be important in ministry, but they are not part of this framework's definition of breakthrough change.

variants.stem: The Mustard Seed Principle from Week 8 draws a parallel between small seeds producing large outcomes and a leadership concept. What is that concept?
options: Leverage — small, well-placed actions that produce disproportionately large Kingdom impact
Delegation — distributing tasks widely so no single leader is overburdened
Incremental planning — taking the smallest possible step to minimize risk
Scaling — growing programs as large as possible to maximize reach

feedback.correct: The Mustard Seed Principle illustrates leverage: just as a tiny seed produces an enormous plant, a small, strategically placed action can generate disproportionately large impact. This is about precision and placement, not about doing less or doing more — it's about doing the right thing in the right place.
feedback.incorrect: The Mustard Seed Principle is specifically about leverage — the idea that impact comes not from the size of the action but from its strategic placement. It's not about delegation, cautious incrementalism, or scaling up. The power is in identifying where a small intervention can shift an entire system.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: In the Community Youth Development (CYD) framework, treating youth solely as recipients of services limits their development and the community's capacity.
questions.reason: The Developmental Pathway Model describes a progression from Youth as Recipients to Youth as Resources, Assets, and ultimately Leaders.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the Developmental Pathway Model directly explains why recipient-only approaches are limiting. If youth can progress through being Resources, Assets, and Leaders, then holding them at the Recipient stage means missing the developmental potential described by the pathway. The model reveals what's lost when ministries only serve youth rather than developing and empowering them.
feedback.incorrect: Both statements are genuine CYD principles, and they are causally connected. The Developmental Pathway Model isn't just a parallel observation — it explains precisely why recipient-only approaches are insufficient. The pathway shows what youth are capable of becoming (Resources, Assets, Leaders), which reveals the developmental loss when ministries cap youth at the Recipient level.

variants.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
variants.assertion: The 'Stop, Start, Continue, Improve' framework is primarily a budgeting tool for allocating financial resources across ministry programs.
variants.reason: Ministry transformation requires evaluating which current activities to eliminate, which new activities to begin, which to maintain, and which to refine.

feedback.correct: The assertion is false — 'Stop, Start, Continue, Improve' is a ministry transformation framework, not a budgeting tool. It helps leaders evaluate all aspects of their ministry practice, not just financial allocation. The reason, however, is true: it accurately describes the four evaluative categories the framework uses. The reason corrects the assertion's misconception about the framework's purpose.
feedback.incorrect: The 'Stop, Start, Continue, Improve' framework is about holistic ministry transformation — examining practices, relationships, programs, and approaches — not just budgets. The reason statement accurately describes the framework's four categories, but the assertion mischaracterizes its purpose as purely financial. One statement is true while the other is false.

questions.stem: In Fast Forward Planning™ from Week 12, a ministry team has identified their Focusing Question and mapped Driving Forces. They now need to develop four alternative scenarios using the Big Grid. Which pair of scenarios represents the two most contrasting futures?
options: Monument (institutional preservation, low community engagement) and Movement (adaptive, high community engagement)
Monument (institutional preservation) and Lifeboat (institutional preservation under stress)
Pioneer (individual innovation) and Movement (collective innovation)
Lifeboat (survival mode) and Pioneer (exploring new territory)
justifications: Monument and Movement sit at opposite corners of the Big Grid — Monument preserves existing institutional forms with minimal community engagement, while Movement represents adaptive, community-engaged ministry, making them the most fundamentally contrasting pair
Monument and Lifeboat are the most contrasting because one involves stability and the other involves crisis, even though both prioritize institutional preservation
Pioneer and Movement are most contrasting because individual innovation and collective innovation are opposite approaches to ministry
Lifeboat and Pioneer contrast because survival and exploration are psychologically opposite orientations to the future

feedback.correct: The Big Grid arranges four scenarios along two axes. Monument and Movement occupy diagonally opposite positions, representing the maximum contrast: Monument is about preserving institutional forms with low community engagement, while Movement is about adaptive, community-engaged ministry. Understanding this contrast helps teams prepare for the widest range of possible futures.
feedback.incorrect: While other pairs do differ, Monument and Movement represent the most fundamental contrast in the Big Grid because they diverge on both key dimensions. Monument-Lifeboat both preserve institutional forms; Pioneer-Movement both emphasize adaptability. Only Monument-Movement diverge maximally across the grid's structure.
feedback.wrongReason: Students sometimes correctly identify Monument and Movement as most contrasting but misexplain why. The key is that they sit at diagonally opposite corners of the Big Grid, differing on both underlying dimensions — not just on one axis. The Big Grid is a two-dimensional framework, and maximum contrast requires divergence on both dimensions.

variants.stem: In the Fast Forward Planning™ process from Week 12, a ministry team is deciding how to act on their four scenarios. According to the strategic priorities framework, which type of action should be taken for strategies that are valuable across all four scenarios?
options: Just Do It — implement immediately because the strategy is robust across all possible futures
Develop Essential Capacities — build foundational skills that will be needed regardless of which scenario unfolds
Prepare Contingency Plans — create backup plans in case one particular scenario materializes
Access Additional Capacities — seek outside expertise for strategies that depend on unpredictable conditions
justifications: Strategies that prove valuable across all four scenarios are no-regret moves — they help regardless of which future materializes, so there is no reason to delay implementation
Even strategies that work in all scenarios should be delayed until the team has developed more foundational skills and organizational readiness
Contingency planning is appropriate for all strategies because the future is inherently uncertain and no strategy can be guaranteed to work
External expertise should be sought for any strategy the team hasn't implemented before, regardless of how robust it appears across scenarios

feedback.correct: The 'Just Do It' category is for strategies that are robust across all four scenarios. Since they add value regardless of which future unfolds, delaying them has no strategic benefit — they are no-regret moves. Contingency Plans are for scenario-specific strategies; Develop Essential Capacities and Access Additional Capacities are for building readiness for less certain futures.
feedback.incorrect: The four strategic priority categories each serve a different function. 'Just Do It' is specifically for strategies robust across all scenarios. Contingency Plans are for scenario-dependent strategies. Essential Capacities involve building foundational readiness. Additional Capacities involve accessing outside resources. Matching the right priority category to the right type of strategy is what makes scenario planning actionable.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may correctly choose 'Just Do It' but then provide a justification about urgency or simplicity rather than the actual logic: these strategies are no-regret moves because they add value across all four possible futures. The justification must be about robustness across scenarios, not about ease of implementation.

prompts.prompt: When a big project, team, or plan completely falls apart — be honest — is your first instinct to blame the PEOPLE involved, or to question the SYSTEM they were working in? Which one is usually more right?

Have you ever been in a group — sports team, friend group, family, job — that kept making the SAME mistake over and over, even after everyone agreed to do better? What do you think was actually going on?
If you could change just ONE rule, policy, or structure at this university to make the biggest possible difference for students — what would it be? Why that one thing specifically?
Hot take: Is it ever actually smarter to just fix the symptom and leave the root cause alone? Or is that always a cop-out?
Think of something you tried recently that didn't go as planned. Did you actually sit down afterward and figure out WHY it went wrong — or did you just move on? Does it even matter?
Which is scarier: a problem that explodes suddenly and everyone can see it — or a problem that quietly hums along in the background for years without anyone noticing?

activities.title: Iceberg Excavation: Digging Below the Event
activities.description: The instructor presents a single, vivid real-world 'event' on screen — for example, 'A hospital's ER wait times have tripled over six months' or 'A tech company's best engineers keep quitting.' Students are split into breakout groups of 4-5 and given a shared digital whiteboard (Miro, FigJam, or a Google Slide template pre-built with the five Iceberg levels labeled). Each group has 10 minutes to populate all five levels — Events, Patterns, Structures, Mental Models, and Intent — working collaboratively in real time on their shared board. Groups then return to the main room and one spokesperson from each does a 60-second 'surface to seafloor' walkthrough of their iceberg. The instructor facilitates a 3-minute debrief comparing where different groups diverged, especially at the Mental Models and Intent levels, highlighting that those lower levels are where the real explanatory power lives.
