activities.materials: Pre-built Iceberg template in Miro, FigJam, or Google Slides (one copy per breakout group); Zoom or similar platform with breakout room functionality
activities.learningGoal: Reinforces the five levels of the Iceberg model and builds practice distinguishing surface events from deeper structural and mental-model causes
activities.scalingNotes: For 25 students, 5 groups of 5 works well. For smaller cohorts (under 15), run 3 groups and spend more debrief time comparing. For larger cohorts (40+), use 6-7 groups and have groups post their boards in a shared folder rather than presenting live — instructor highlights 2-3 contrasting examples.

activities.title: The Leverage Point Showdown
activities.description: The instructor presents a complex systems scenario (e.g., declining student engagement in a school district, a supply chain that keeps breaking down) and lists 6-8 possible interventions on a shared screen — ranging from shallow fixes (add more staff, run a campaign) to deeper structural or mental-model shifts (redesign incentive structures, rewrite the core mission). Students first vote individually using a live poll (Mentimeter, Slido, or Zoom poll) on which intervention they believe is the highest-leverage point. Results are displayed and will almost certainly be split. The instructor then assigns students to breakout pairs or trios where one person argued for option A and one for option B, forcing cross-position dialogue for 5 minutes: 'Make the strongest case for your choice using systems language.' Pairs return and the instructor cold-calls 3-4 students to share what their partner's argument almost convinced them of. The session closes with the instructor using Donella Meadows' leverage point hierarchy to frame which interventions tend to create lasting change versus quick but shallow fixes.
activities.materials: Live polling tool (Mentimeter or Slido — free tier sufficient); scenario and intervention list shared on screen as a slide
activities.learningGoal: Builds intuition for identifying high-leverage intervention points and challenges the instinct to default to event-level fixes
activities.scalingNotes: Works well at any size with polling. For 25 students, assign breakout pairs (12-13 rooms). For larger groups, use trios. If the poll produces a clear majority for one answer, ask the minority to double down and steelman their position — this generates richer debate.

activities.title: After-Action Review in the Hot Seat
activities.description: Before this activity, the instructor asks 1-2 student volunteers (recruited beforehand, ideally) to bring a real recent experience — a project that went sideways, a team decision that backfired, or an initiative that underperformed — to use as a live AAR case. The full group acts as the AAR facilitating team, working through the six steps together: Intent, Actual Results, Gap Analysis, Lessons Learned, Shared Vision, and Next Steps. The instructor displays the AAR framework on screen and acts as the process guide while students drive the questions in the chat or via unmuted responses. A designated 'scribe' student captures responses in a shared Google Doc projected on screen. The goal is not to 'fix' the volunteer's situation but to model how the AAR structure surfaces systemic lessons rather than individual blame. The debrief asks: 'At which step did we most naturally slip into blaming events or people instead of structures?'
activities.materials: AAR framework slide displayed on screen; shared Google Doc for live capture (instructor shares link in chat); one pre-briefed student volunteer with a real case
activities.learningGoal: Applies the full AAR framework to a real situation and builds skill in moving from gap analysis to structural lessons learned
activities.scalingNotes: Works well with 15-35 students — the live doc gives everyone a participation channel even if they don't unmute. For very large groups (50+), use a moderated Q&A format where students submit questions via chat and the instructor selects and voices them. Always brief your volunteer ahead of time and emphasize psychological safety framing at the start.

activities.title: Capacity Limit Mapping: What's Actually Holding the System Back?
activities.description: The instructor introduces a scenario in which a system appears to be 'trying' to improve but keeps hitting invisible ceilings — for example, a nonprofit that trains more volunteers but still can't scale its impact, or a software team that adopts Agile but misses every sprint. Students work in breakout groups of 5 for 8 minutes, using a simple shared Google Doc grid with two columns: 'Forces/Actions Inside the System' and 'How They Contribute to the Undesired Result.' Their task is to brainstorm as many capacity-limiting forces as possible — structural, resource-based, behavioral, cultural — and map them to the undesired outcome. Groups return and share their top two most surprising or counterintuitive constraints. The instructor then asks the whole group: 'Which of these limits could be addressed at the event level? Which require going deeper?' This sets up the connection between capacity limits and leverage points.
activities.materials: Shared Google Doc template with the two-column grid (one per group, links dropped in breakout room chat); scenario slide displayed before breakout
activities.learningGoal: Builds fluency in identifying capacity-limiting forces inside a system and connecting them to undesired systemic outcomes
activities.scalingNotes: 5 groups of 5 is ideal for 25 students. For smaller cohorts, use 3 groups and have each group present a fuller walkthrough. For 40+ students, groups of 6-7 work fine — the collaborative doc keeps everyone contributing simultaneously rather than waiting to speak.

activities.title: Mental Model Museum: Making the Invisible Visible
activities.description: This activity targets the fourth level of the Iceberg — Mental Models — which students typically find the hardest to surface because it requires turning assumptions into explicit statements. The instructor opens a shared Miro board with six 'exhibit frames' on it, each containing a brief description of a real organizational or social system (e.g., 'A company that hasn't changed its performance review process in 20 years despite poor results'). Working individually for 3 minutes, each student places sticky notes on 2-3 exhibits answering the prompt: 'What belief or assumption must people inside this system hold for this structure to feel normal or necessary?' Students then do a 5-minute gallery walk — scrolling through each other's sticky notes on the same Miro board and adding a star emoji to any assumption that surprises or resonates with them. The instructor then facilitates a 5-minute whole-group discussion surfacing the most-starred assumptions, asking: 'How do these mental models protect themselves from being challenged, and what would it take to shift them?'
activities.materials: Pre-built Miro board with 6 exhibit frames and sticky note tool enabled; students need Miro access (free account works)
activities.learningGoal: Develops the skill of surfacing and articulating hidden mental models — the most powerful and most elusive level of the Iceberg — and connects them to persistent structural patterns
activities.scalingNotes: Miro handles 25 simultaneous users well on the free tier. For very large groups, limit active exhibits to 3-4 and create duplicate boards so no single board gets too cluttered. For smaller, more intimate cohorts, replace the gallery walk with a brief verbal round-robin where each student reads their most interesting sticky note aloud.

activities.title: Systems Sprint: Full Iceberg to Intervention in 12 Minutes
activities.description: This is a fast-paced synthesis activity best used near the end of the session to integrate all major concepts. The instructor presents a fresh, unfamiliar scenario (different from any used earlier in class) and each student has exactly 5 minutes working solo in a personal Google Doc or digital notepad to run the full sequence: map the Iceberg (all five levels in brief), identify the top capacity limit, name one high-leverage intervention point, and write one-sentence AAR-style intent statement for that intervention. At the 5-minute mark, students are paired randomly in breakout rooms for 5 minutes to compare their analyses — not to reach agreement, but to find where their mental models about the system diverged. The session closes in the main room with the instructor asking 3-4 students: 'What did your partner see that you completely missed?' This surfaces how differently even well-trained observers frame the same system — and reinforces why shared vision in the AAR process matters.
activities.materials: Fresh scenario slide (do not reuse earlier examples); students use their own personal notes doc or a provided Google Doc template
activities.learningGoal: Integrates all four key concepts — the Iceberg model, AAR, capacity limits, and leverage points — into a single rapid-cycle application, and reveals how individual mental models shape systemic diagnosis
activities.scalingNotes: Random pairing in Zoom breakout rooms works seamlessly for 25 students (12 pairs, one trio). For larger groups, keep the same structure — random pairs scale infinitely. For cohorts under 12, skip the breakout and run the comparison as a full-group share-out in the main room.

slides.title: The Iceberg Below the Waterline
slides.bodyText: If you only react to what you can see, you will always be surprised by what you can't.
slides.speakerNotes: Welcome everyone. Today's session is about a fundamental shift in how you see problems — and organizations, and teams, and systems. The Titanic didn't sink because of the iceberg above the water. It sank because of what was hidden. Most management failures work the same way. By the end of this session, you'll have three tools to see deeper: the Systems Iceberg, Leverage Points, and the After-Action Review. Let's dive in.

slides.title: Seeing the Whole System
slides.bodyText: We explore why smart people keep solving the wrong problems — and what to do about it.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition from orientation to the first big framework. Most students come in thinking systems thinking is abstract theory. Reframe it immediately: this is a practical diagnostic skill. Ask the class: 'Has anyone ever fixed a problem, only to have it come back worse?' That's systems blindness. That's what this section addresses.

slides.title: The Systems Iceberg: Five Levels
bullets: Events — what just happened? (visible, reactive)
Patterns — trends over time; what keeps happening?
Structures — rules, incentives, processes driving patterns
Mental Models — beliefs and assumptions behind the structures
Intent — the purpose or vision the whole system serves
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through each level slowly. Events are the news — the fire alarm going off. Patterns are the fire alarms go off every third Tuesday. Structures are the wiring in the building, or the incentives that lead to shortcuts. Mental models are 'we've always done it this way' or 'that's just how this industry works.' Intent is what the system is ultimately optimized for — which may not be what anyone consciously chose. Ask: where do most organizations spend their time? (Events.) Where should they spend more time? (Structures and Mental Models.)

slides.title: The Key Insight
slides.bodyText: Every recurring problem is a systems problem in disguise — and you can't fix a systems problem from the event level.
slides.speakerNotes: Pause here. Let this land. Ask students to write down one recurring problem in their organization or life. Then ask: which level of the iceberg have they been trying to fix it at? The insight is almost always that they've been swatting at events while the structure underneath keeps generating new ones. This is the photograph moment — the one they'll remember.

slides.title: Peter Senge, The Fifth Discipline
slides.speakerNotes: Senge wrote this in 1990 and it still stops people cold. Ask the class to think of an example — a policy introduced to fix one problem that created three more. Classic examples: performance bonuses that destroy collaboration, safety rules that create workarounds, org restructures that just move dysfunction around. This quote is the Systems Iceberg in nine words. The 'solution' was applied at the event level; the underlying structure was untouched.

slides.title: Capacity Limits & Leverage
slides.bodyText: We examine the hidden forces that keep systems stuck — and the rare points where a small push creates lasting change.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition language: 'Now that we know how to SEE the iceberg, the next question is — where do we touch it? Not every part of a system responds the same way to intervention. Some parts absorb your effort and give nothing back. Others are like a fulcrum — move them a little, change everything.' This section connects the Iceberg model to actionable intervention theory.

slides.title: Capacity Limits: Why Systems Stay Stuck
bullets: Forces or actions that generate undesired results
Often invisible — embedded in structure, not behavior
Examples: bottlenecks, missing skills, conflicting incentives
Pushing harder against a capacity limit rarely works
Must be identified before leverage points can be found
slides.speakerNotes: Capacity limits are the reasons your team hits the same wall repeatedly. They're not laziness or bad attitude — they're structural. Give a concrete example: a hospital ER that keeps missing discharge targets. The capacity limit isn't nurse effort; it's the bed-assignment process upstream. Pushing nurses harder doesn't fix bed assignments. Ask students: what's a capacity limit you've personally run into at work? Get 2-3 responses and map them to Iceberg levels.

slides.title: Symptom Fix vs. System Fix
bullets: Hire more people for the bottleneck
Run a one-time training sprint
Punish individuals for system failures
Add rules on top of broken rules
slides.bodyText: ["Redesign the process creating the bottleneck", "Build structural learning into the workflow", "Redesign incentives that caused the behavior", "Change the mental model behind the rule"]
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through each pair side by side. The left column feels like action; it's satisfying in the moment. The right column is harder, slower, and creates lasting change. The key question to ask of any proposed fix: 'Does this address the structure, or just the symptom?' Also note that right-column solutions usually require identifying the mental models driving the structures — which is why the Iceberg model matters before you get here.

slides.title: Leverage Points: Where Small Moves Matter
bullets: Not all interventions are equal — some have 10x impact
Low leverage: adjusting numbers, tweaking parameters
Medium leverage: changing rules, restructuring flows
High leverage: shifting goals, altering feedback loops
Highest leverage: changing the mental models themselves
slides.speakerNotes: Reference Donella Meadows here — her landmark essay 'Leverage Points: Places to Intervene in a System' (1999) is the foundational text. The counterintuitive finding: most organizations spend 90% of their energy at the low-leverage end. Adjusting budgets, tweaking metrics. Meanwhile the mental models driving the whole structure go untouched. Ask: 'If you could change ONE mental model in your organization, what would it be?' This typically generates the richest discussion of the session.

slides.title: Donella Meadows, Leverage Points (1999)
slides.bodyText: "The highest leverage point of all is to keep oneself unattached to the positions one takes in a system — to stay flexible, to realize that no paradigm is 'true.'"
slides.speakerNotes: This one is deliberately challenging. Students who are used to confident decision-making frameworks may push back — 'Aren't we supposed to have strong convictions?' The key distinction is between commitment to outcomes and attachment to methods and mental models. The best systems thinkers hold their models lightly. They're always asking: what would have to be true for my current mental model to be wrong? Pause after reading it. Let the discomfort sit for 10 seconds before speaking.

slides.title: Learning From Action
slides.bodyText: We look at the After-Action Review — a structured method for turning experience into systemic learning rather than blame or amnesia.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition: 'Systems thinking tells us WHERE to look. Now we need a process for HOW to learn from what we find. The After-Action Review, originally developed by the U.S. Army, is one of the most powerful learning tools ever designed — and one of the most routinely botched.' Acknowledge that most students have experienced bad AARs: either blame sessions or feel-good retrospectives that change nothing. Today we look at why, and how to do it right.

slides.title: The After-Action Review: Six Steps
bullets: Intent — what were we trying to achieve?
Actual Results — what actually happened? (facts, not judgment)
Gap Analysis — where did intent and results diverge, and why?
Lessons Learned — what does the system need to change?
Shared Vision — what future are we now aligned to build?
Next Steps — who does what, by when?
slides.speakerNotes: Emphasize Step 1 (Intent) and Step 3 (Gap Analysis) as the most skipped. Teams often jump straight to 'what went wrong' without first establishing shared clarity on what success was supposed to look like — which means the gap analysis becomes a debate about goals, not a diagnosis of the system. Connect to the Iceberg: the AAR should be asking Gap Analysis questions at the Structure and Mental Model levels, not just the Event level. Otherwise you get lessons like 'communicate better' — which changes nothing.

slides.title: The AAR Failure Mode
slides.bodyText: An After-Action Review that stops at 'what happened' is just a story. Learning begins when you ask what in the system made that outcome almost inevitable.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the synthesis moment of the session. The AAR is only as powerful as the Iceberg framework it operates within. Without the Iceberg, AARs become postmortems of events. With it, they become structural diagnostics. Ask students: 'Think of the last retrospective or after-action you participated in. What level of the Iceberg did the conversation mostly happen at?' Almost universally: Events. That's the gap this course is trying to close.

slides.title: Iceberg + AAR + Leverage: How They Connect
bullets: Iceberg: diagnose which level the problem lives at
Capacity limits: identify what's holding the system back
Leverage points: find where to intervene most effectively
AAR: structured reflection that drives systemic learning
Together: a cycle of seeing, acting, and learning whole
slides.speakerNotes: This is the integration slide. Don't rush it. Draw the loop on the board if possible: the AAR feeds new observations into the Iceberg analysis, which surfaces new leverage points, which inform better-designed interventions, which generate new material for the next AAR. This is what a learning organization actually looks like in practice — not a poster on the wall, but this cycle running continuously. Ask: 'Where does your organization currently break this loop?'

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Most problems are solved at the wrong level — the Iceberg shows you where to look deeper
Capacity limits are structural, not personal — pushing harder rarely helps
Highest leverage interventions target mental models and feedback loops
An AAR without structural analysis is just storytelling
Systems thinkers stay curious about their own assumptions
slides.speakerNotes: Close by asking students to write down one thing they'll do differently in the next week using today's frameworks. Not a grand intervention — just one moment where they'll pause before reacting to an event and ask 'which level of the iceberg is this really living at?' That micro-habit, practiced consistently, is how systems thinking actually takes root. Preview next session briefly, then open for questions.

blocks.title: Audiobook
blocks.transcript:
Pastor Randall Jones stood at the front of the room holding a single sheet of paper — the financial summary for the Jackson Youth Outreach three-on-three Basketball Tournament. Four years ago, he had championed this project. It had started as a lean, community-rooted outreach to teenage boys who would never walk through a church door on their own. In Year One, ten young men prayed a first-time commitment prayer after a simple closing message. The room celebrated. By Year Four, two thousand people were crowding the Saturday night concert, a local radio station was broadcasting live, and the deficit was ten thousand dollars. Commitments: ten — exactly the same as Year One, despite a crowd two hundred times larger.

Nobody intended this. Nobody woke up on any single morning and decided to transform a ministry into an entertainment production. The drift happened gradually, invisibly, driven by forces no one named and no one saw clearly enough to address. That invisibility is not a failure of intention. It is a failure of seeing — a failure to look below the waterline at the structures, mental models, and drifting intent that were silently reshaping everything above the surface. This session is about learning to see differently.

[short pause]

Every experienced ministry leader can recall a variation of this experience: a conflict resolved, only to reemerge in a different relationship six months later. A funding crisis addressed, only to return when a key donor steps back. A program redesigned, only to produce the same attendance patterns as before. Peter Senge identified this phenomenon as one of the defining characteristics of organizations that have not yet learned to think systemically — in his 1990 work, he observed that leaders tend to treat events as if they were causes rather than symptoms.

Senge's foundational argument in The Fifth Discipline is that most organizational problems do not originate in the events we observe but in the deeper structures that produce them. When leaders react only to events — patching the symptom without understanding the underlying dynamic — the problem invariably resurfaces, often in a more entrenched form. He calls this EVENT-LEVEL THINKING: the reflexive tendency to see an isolated occurrence, respond to it, declare it resolved, and move on, never asking what structural force created the event in the first place.

This is not a leadership failure unique to poor leaders. It is actually the natural default for intelligent, well-intentioned people under pressure — people very much like the Jackson Youth Outreach organizing team. As Donella Meadows argued in Thinking in Systems, published in 2008, "The behavior of a system cannot be known just by knowing the elements of which the system is made." What drives behavior is structure — the relationships among elements, the feedback loops operating between them, the policies and norms that constrain how they interact. Systems reliably produce the results they are designed to produce, whether or not those results were ever consciously chosen.

[short pause]

Take a moment to bring to mind one problem in your own ministry that you have addressed at least twice. What did you do each time? What came back? Without yet having a name for it, where do you suspect the real source of that recurring problem actually lives?

[short pause]

The most useful conceptual tool for this kind of seeing is the SYSTEMS ICEBERG, adapted for organizational contexts by Daniel Kim and Peter Senge. The iceberg is an apt metaphor: approximately ninety percent of an iceberg's mass lies below the waterline, invisible to observers on the surface. The same is true of the forces driving organizational behavior. The visible ten percent — what we observe day to day — is shaped and sustained by a vast, largely unseen substructure, as both Senge and Jamshid Gharajedaghi have noted in their respective works on systems thinking.

The iceberg has five levels, each deeper and more powerful than the one above it.

[short pause]

The first level is Events. Events are what we observe on the surface: the things that happen to us, the crises we respond to, the anomalies we notice. In the Jackson Youth Outreach case, the events are unmistakable — a ten-thousand-dollar budget deficit, declining commitment numbers, exhausted volunteers, community opposition, and a founding leader announcing his resignation from the organizing team. Events demand our attention. They arrive with urgency and emotional charge. And because they are visible, they are where most leaders spend most of their energy.

The problem with reacting exclusively at the events level is not that events are unimportant; it is that they are uninformative about causation. An event tells you that something has gone wrong. It rarely tells you why.

[short pause]

The second level is PATTERNS. When we zoom out from a single event and ask whether it is part of a recurring trend, we move to the patterns level. Patterns reveal that what looked like an isolated event is actually a predictable consequence of a system doing exactly what it was set up to do. In the Jackson Youth Outreach case, a patterns-level observation would notice that commitments plateaued in Year Two and never recovered, that budget overruns increased in each of Years Three and Four, that community opposition grew in direct proportion to event scale, and that paid staff time shifted progressively away from discipleship toward event production. None of these is a one-time occurrence. Each is a trend — a system behaving consistently over time.

Recognizing patterns is an essential act of leadership humility. As Senge and colleagues observed in their 1999 work The Dance of Change, it requires acknowledging that the current situation is not simply bad luck or a one-off failure — it is a revelation about how the organization is structured.

[short pause]

The third level is Structures. Below patterns lie the STRUCTURES that produce them: the policies, norms, relationships, physical arrangements, incentive systems, and organizational habits that are actually driving behavior. Structures are not purely formal; many of the most powerful are informal — the unwritten rules that govern how meetings run, how credit is distributed, how disagreement is managed, how success is measured.

In the Jackson Youth Outreach case, key structural elements include the decision to hire paid staff for event production rather than volunteer development; the use of attendance rather than discipleship depth as the primary measure of success; the organizational momentum that came from a growing budget and growing crowd; and the gradual inclusion of suburban churches whose community development commitments differed from the founding urban partners. These structural forces did not announce themselves. They accumulated quietly, each year's decision building on the last, until the structure of the organization bore no resemblance to the structure of its founding vision — a point Gharajedaghi makes compellingly in his 2011 work on systems thinking.

[short pause]

The fourth level is MENTAL MODELS. Structures do not emerge from nowhere. They are built and sustained by mental models — the deeply held beliefs, assumptions, and values that shape how leaders see the world and what they regard as possible, desirable, or even thinkable. The mental model concept originates in cognitive science, where Philip Johnson-Laird demonstrated in 1983 that human reasoning is mediated by internal representations — simplified maps of the world that we use to make sense of experience and predict outcomes.

In organizational contexts, Senge argues that mental models are the invisible governors of structure. In his words, "Mental models are deeply ingrained assumptions, generalizations, or even pictures or images that influence how we understand the world and how we take action." The mental models driving the Jackson Youth Outreach's structural drift likely included beliefs such as: bigger crowds equal more Kingdom impact; community visibility legitimates ministry effectiveness; entertainment is a valid bridge to evangelism; growth without limit is inherently good. None of these beliefs was necessarily wrong in isolation. But operating unchallenged and unexamined, they silently authorized every structural decision that moved the organization away from its missional core.

[short pause]

At the deepest level of the iceberg lies Intent. The fifth and foundational level is INTENT — the foundational purpose or goal that originally shaped the mental models and that continues, or fails to continue, to animate everything above it. Intent is not mission statement language. It is the lived, deeply personal conviction that drew the founders to this work in the first place — what Senge calls the personal vision that, when shared, becomes an organization's animating energy.

The Jackson Youth Outreach's founding intent was specific and powerful: to reach teenage boys who would never enter a church, through a community-rooted, relationships-based outreach. David Williams proposed it not as a production but as a ministry of presence — coaches mentoring youth, volunteers coming alongside team members, a modest Saturday event with an evangelistic message. That intent was not abandoned overnight. It was eroded slowly by the weight of success metrics that pointed in a different direction. When we examine the organization at the end of Year Four and ask, "What is this organization actually trying to accomplish?", the honest answer bears little resemblance to the founding intent. This gap between founding purpose and operational reality is what scholars of organizational learning call STRUCTURAL DRIFT.

[short pause]

One of the most practically useful concepts in systems thinking is what the DVULI curriculum identifies as capacity limits — the forces or actions within a system that contribute to undesired results, often despite the best intentions of participants. Meadows describes these as the constraints built into a system's structure that prevent it from achieving the outcomes its participants desire.

Capacity limits are particularly treacherous because they are invisible from inside the system. The Jackson Youth Outreach's organizing team never decided to become an entertainment production company. What they did, repeatedly and reasonably, was respond to the visible pressures in front of them: declining commitments in Year Three prompted them to book better entertainment; budget shortfalls prompted larger fundraising targets; community criticism prompted more police presence rather than a reconsideration of scale. Each response made local sense. Each response also reinforced the structural forces that were producing the very problems being addressed — what Senge calls a classic "fixes that fail" pattern.

Meadows identified twelve leverage points for intervening in a system, ranked by their potential effectiveness, in a 1999 essay. Her key finding was counterintuitive: the interventions leaders most commonly attempt — adjusting numbers, tweaking policies, reorganizing structures — are among the least effective. The most powerful leverage points involve changing the goals of the system, the paradigms or mental models out of which the system arises, and the power to change those paradigms themselves. As Meadows wrote, "The higher the leverage point, the more the system will resist changing it." This explains why the Jackson Youth Outreach's annual tactical adjustments never produced systemic change: they were operating at the lowest-leverage levels of the system.

[short pause]

As Senge expressed it in his foundational work, the primary task of systems leadership is not to manage events but to design the systems and structures that govern organizational behavior. For ministry leaders, this reorientation toward high-leverage intervention carries a deeply biblical resonance. The DVULI Core Value of Leverage — the capacity for seemingly small actions to have disproportionately large effects — is most fully realized when those actions target the structural and mental-model levels of the iceberg rather than the event level. Nehemiah understood this intuitively: before he touched a single stone in Jerusalem's walls, he mapped the political, social, and resource systems he would need to engage. As Kenneth Boa observed in 2005, Nehemiah worked at the level of structure and relationship, not crisis management.

[short pause]

Systems thinking without structured collective learning, however, remains an intellectual exercise. The AFTER-ACTION REVIEW is the practical bridge between systemic insight and organizational change. Developed by the United States Army Research Institute as a systematic method for military units to debrief after operations, the After-Action Review creates a structured space in which teams can examine what they intended to accomplish, what actually happened, why the gap exists, and how to move forward together — a framework documented by Weick and Roberts in 1993.

Senge and colleagues adapted the After-Action Review framework for organizational learning contexts in The Dance of Change, arguing that it addresses one of the primary obstacles to sustained organizational transformation: the failure to institutionalize learning from experience. Without a disciplined process for collective reflection, organizations repeat not just their successes but their failure modes — the same capacity limits reassert themselves, the same mental models redirect the next initiative, the same structural drift resumes its quiet work.

[short pause]

The After-Action Review has six steps, each designed to move the team through a complete learning cycle.

The first step is Intent — what did we set out to do, and why? This step establishes the original purpose as the reference point for all subsequent evaluation. It requires honesty about founding intent rather than retrospective reframing.

The second step is Actual Results — what really happened? Both positive and negative outcomes must be named specifically, grounded in evidence rather than impression. This step often surfaces information that circulates informally but has never been spoken aloud collectively.

The third step is Gap Analysis — why did our actual results differ from our intent? This is the heart of the After-Action Review and the point at which systems thinking becomes essential. The gap between intent and result is almost never explained by the immediate preceding actions; it is almost always explained by structural and mental-model level forces that accumulated over time.

The fourth step is Lessons Learned — what generalized insights can we extract? Lessons that remain specific to a single incident have limited value; the goal is to identify transferable principles about how this system behaves under these conditions.

The fifth step is Shared Vision — how can we move forward together? This step re-grounds the team in a common commitment to the future, preventing the After-Action Review from becoming a blame exercise. It reconnects the group to the founding intent that first animated their work.

The sixth step is Next Steps — what must we do, and by when? Without specific commitments, even the most insightful After-Action Review dissolves into good intentions. Next steps must be assigned, dated, and followed up.

[short pause]

Applying the After-Action Review framework to the Jackson Youth Outreach case at the DVULI Second National Conference revealed something important: nearly every stakeholder group had pieces of the analysis that no other group possessed. Urban pastors understood the community relationships that had been fractured; event staff could see the operational pressures that had redirected resources; founding volunteers articulated the spiritual vision that had been crowded out; suburban church representatives named the demographic assumptions that had shaped programming choices. No individual perspective contained the whole picture. The After-Action Review, at its best, creates the conditions for that whole picture to emerge collectively.

[short pause]

Think about a significant ministry initiative from the past two years. Could you articulate, clearly and honestly, all six steps of an After-Action Review for that initiative right now — especially the Gap Analysis? What would you need to know or gather to do so? Who would need to be in the room for the full picture to be visible?

[short pause]

Armed with the Iceberg framework and the After-Action Review, the ministry leader faces a final and critical question: at what level do I now intervene? Senge's answer, stated plainly in his essay "The Leader's New Work," is that the primary work of leaders in learning organizations is not to manage events but to design the systems and structures that shape organizational behavior. He distinguishes between event-level leadership — reactive crisis management — and systemic leadership — the deliberate design of enabling structures.

For the Jackson Youth Outreach, a systemic intervention would not focus primarily on booking a better speaker for Year Five or restructuring budget line items. It would ask structural and mental-model questions: What would it mean to redesign the tournament's success metrics to center discipleship depth rather than attendance? What norms would need to change to make that measurement visible and valued? What mental models about ministry effectiveness are being reinforced by the current structure, and how might we surface and examine them collectively?

This is exactly the territory that the After-Action Review, applied rigorously and honestly, is designed to map. When Pastor Jones proposed a community dialogue as the response to the Year Four crisis, he was — intuitively — creating the conditions for a collective After-Action Review. The question was whether the dialogue would remain at the events and patterns levels, asking things like "attendance is down, let's fix the program," or whether it would dive into the structural and mental-model levels where actual transformation becomes possible.

Gharajedaghi emphasizes that systems thinking is not primarily an analytical technique but a design discipline — a way of seeing that enables more intentional intervention. The Iceberg model and the After-Action Review are not ends in themselves; they are tools for generating the quality of insight that makes structural redesign possible rather than merely reactive patching. Together, they form the systems leadership toolkit that the DVULI Leverage Core Value points toward: the disciplined capacity to find the high-leverage places in the ministry system where small, intentional changes produce large, sustainable transformation.

[short pause]

It is worth pausing to note the profound spiritual resonance of what systems thinking asks of us as leaders. The biblical invitation to be transformed by the renewing of your mind, from the twelfth chapter of Romans, is at its core an invitation to examine and revise the mental models — the deeply held assumptions and worldviews — through which we see ourselves, others, and the world. The tribe of Issachar offers a scriptural archetype for systems leadership: men who "understood the times and knew what Israel should do," as recorded in First Chronicles chapter twelve. To understand the times is to see below the surface of events, to recognize patterns, to identify the structural forces shaping the moment, and to act with wisdom rather than mere urgency.

Nehemiah's leadership through the rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls is a masterclass in iceberg-level thinking applied in real time. He did not simply gather stones. He mapped the political systems, secured structural permissions, built relational coalitions, and attended to the mental models of both allies and opponents before any visible construction began. The wall was built not by reacting to opposition but by designing a system of response, community, and accountable leadership that could sustain itself through opposition.

For urban ministry leaders operating in communities shaped by generations of structural injustice and systemic neglect, this kind of deep-level seeing is not merely professionally advantageous — it is an act of love. Heather Gordon's observation in the Jackson Youth Outreach dialogue — "When did this become all about the big show?" — was not a program critique. It was a lament for a community whose real needs were being obscured by metrics that measured crowd size rather than transformed lives. Systems thinking restores the clarity to see those real needs, and the analytical tools to design interventions that genuinely address them.

[short pause]

Let's draw together the essential threads of what we have explored. Most recurring organizational problems originate not in events but in the deeper structural and mental-model levels of the Systems Iceberg. Reacting at the event level alone guarantees the problem will return. The five levels of the iceberg — Events, Patterns, Structures, Mental Models, and Intent — provide a navigational map for locating the real source of organizational behavior and the highest-leverage point for intervention.

Capacity limits are the structural forces within a system that consistently produce undesired results despite good intentions, and naming them is the first step to addressing them. Meadows' leverage point framework confirms that the interventions leaders most commonly attempt are among the least effective — changing mental models and system goals produces the most durable transformation.

The After-Action Review creates a structured, six-step process for collective learning that prevents organizations from repeating failure modes, but only if it is applied honestly at all five iceberg levels, not just at the surface of events. And systems leadership — designing the structures that shape behavior rather than managing the events those structures produce — is the highest form of the DVULI Core Value of Leverage in organizational practice.

At its deepest level, the biblical invitation to be transformed by the renewing of the mind is an invitation to examine and revise the mental models that govern how we see and act in the world. That is, finally, what systems thinking asks of us — not a technique, but a conversion of sight.

[short pause]

The next session turns from systems insight to organizational alignment — examining how the structures and strategies of your ministry can be redesigned to embody the vision you have been developing. You will apply the Breakthrough Planning framework to the structural redesign questions this session has surfaced, moving from iceberg analysis to concrete, accountable action steps that target the high-leverage levels of your particular ministry system. Bring your reflections on the After-Action Review and the iceberg levels we have explored — they will form the foundation of your next breakthrough conversation.

blocks.title: Infographic
blocks.prompt:
Create a polished educational infographic in 16:9 landscape format with a light warm background (#faf8f5). The infographic is titled "THE SYSTEMS ICEBERG" in bold header text colored #00558C at the top left, with a smaller subtitle beneath reading "Five Levels of Systemic Understanding" in #334155.

The left two-thirds of the infographic features a large, elegant iceberg diagram oriented vertically. A horizontal wavy blue waterline divides the iceberg into visible and hidden portions. The visible tip above the waterline is small and colored light blue-gray. The massive submerged portion below the waterline grows wider toward the bottom and is rendered in progressively deeper shades of #00558C.

The iceberg has five clearly labeled horizontal layers from top to bottom, each with a short descriptor and a small icon:

Layer 1 (above waterline, smallest): "EVENTS" with label "What happened?" and a lightning bolt icon, lightest shade
Layer 2 (just below waterline): "PATTERNS" with label "What trends recur?" and a wave icon, medium-light #00558C
Layer 3 (mid-depth): "STRUCTURES" with label "What rules shape behavior?" and a gear icon, medium #00558C
Layer 4 (deeper): "MENTAL MODELS" with label "What assumptions drive us?" and a brain icon, darker #00558C
Layer 5 (deepest, widest): "INTENT" with label "What is the deeper purpose?" and a compass icon, darkest #00558C

A thin dotted line at the waterline is labeled "VISIBLE" above and "HIDDEN" below in small caps.

On the right third of the infographic, create two stacked informational panels:

Top panel with a #319B41 left border, titled "LEVERAGE POINTS" in #319B41 bold text. Below, show a small diagram of a lever and fulcrum icon, with three bullet points: "Intervene at deeper levels", "Minimum effort → Maximum change", "Shift structures & mental models, not just events". A curved arrow points from this panel back toward the deeper layers of the iceberg.

Bottom panel with a #d97706 left border, titled "AFTER-ACTION REVIEW" in #d97706 bold text. Show six steps as a compact numbered vertical flow with small connecting arrows: "1. Intent", "2. Actual Results", "3. Gap Analysis", "4. Lessons Learned", "5. Shared Vision", "6. Next Steps". Each step label is short and in dark text.

At the very bottom of the infographic, a thin banner reads: "Capacity limits are the hidden forces within a system that constrain results — found below the waterline" in small italicized #64748b text.

Use clean sans-serif typography throughout. The overall style is minimal, modern, and professional with generous white space. Color palette strictly uses #00558C as primary, #319B41 as secondary, #d97706 as warm accent, with #0f172a for dark text and #64748b for muted text on the #faf8f5 background. No photographic elements — only flat vector-style shapes, icons, and clean lines.

config.concept: Systems Iceberg model: five levels of systemic understanding
config.description: An animated iceberg widget where participants begin at the tip (Events) and click downward through each of the five levels. At each level, they are presented with a specific scenario (the JYO Basketball Tournament case) and prompted to describe what that level reveals about the situation. At the Events level: what do we observe? At the Patterns level: what has been recurring over multiple years? At the Structures level: what policies, relationships, or norms are producing this pattern? At the Mental Models level: what beliefs about success, visibility, and ministry effectiveness are driving the structures? At the Intent level: what was the original purpose, and how has it drifted? The widget generates a completed Iceberg diagram that participants can share and discuss.
config.rationale: The animated depth-dive from surface events to deep mental models embeds the key insight that solutions applied at the Event level will not address the structural causes — participants experience the growing complexity as they descend, encoding through sequential discovery the reason why systems-level interventions require greater depth of inquiry.

config.concept: After-Action Review as collective learning tool
config.description: A six-panel guided worksheet widget structured around the AAR framework. Participants choose either the JYO Basketball Tournament case or a real situation from their own ministry and complete each panel: Intent (What did we set out to do and why?), Actual Results (What really happened — both positive and negative?), Gap Analysis (Why did our actual results differ from our intent?), Lessons Learned (What generalized insights can we extract?), Shared Vision (How can we move forward together?), Next Steps (What must we do and by when?). Each panel has a minimum word count and a prompt to anchor the response in specific evidence rather than opinion. A completed AAR can be exported as a facilitator guide for use with real ministry teams.
config.rationale: Applying the AAR framework to either a familiar case study or a real personal situation ensures that participants develop procedural fluency with the tool — they should leave this session able to facilitate an actual AAR with their ministry team, not merely describe how AAR works.

config.concept: Six Thinking Hats: deliberately expanding the range of perspective in complex decisions
config.description: An interactive role-play widget presenting a complex ministry decision (e.g., whether to partner with a secular community organization on a youth program that reaches more youth but requires compromise on explicit faith content). Six virtual chairs are displayed, each labeled with a different Thinking Hat color (White: Data, Red: Feelings, Green: Creative alternatives, Gray: Caution and risks, Yellow: Benefits and optimism, Blue: Process control). Participants click each hat in sequence and type the response that hat demands — a specific type of thinking about the scenario. The widget enforces hat discipline (e.g., the White Hat prompt will not accept opinion-based responses) and generates a full six-hat analysis that reveals blind spots in decision-making typically dominated by one or two hat styles.
config.rationale: The hat-enforcement feature — which accepts only the appropriate type of thinking for each hat — teaches the discipline of deliberately separating thinking modes rather than conflating them, embedding the core insight that rigorous decision-making requires intentional perspective-shifting.
